article.title: OpenAI, NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, Intel and Microsoft Publish MRC 1.0 Through the OCP, an 800 Gb/s Multipath RDMA Protocol for AI Training
article.summary: Multipath Reliable Connection lets a single RDMA connection spread traffic across multiple network paths and reroute around failures in microseconds. It is already deployed at OCI Abilene and Microsoft Fairwater on NVIDIA Spectrum-X.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

OpenAI, NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, Intel and Microsoft have jointly released Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) as an open specification through the Open Compute Project, NVIDIA's [Gilad Shainer wrote](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/) on the NVIDIA blog. The protocol is already running in production on OpenAI's largest training clusters at the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure site in Abilene, Texas and at Microsoft's Fairwater supercomputers, [Network World reported](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html).

MRC is positioned as a next-generation RDMA transport that lets a single connection distribute traffic across many network paths simultaneously, with hardware-accelerated rerouting when a link or device fails, [according to NVIDIA](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/).

## What MRC Is

In conventional RDMA over Converged Ethernet, a queue pair is bound to a single network path; if that path becomes congested or fails, the connection slows or stalls until software-based recovery completes. MRC instead lets a single RDMA connection distribute traffic across multiple network paths simultaneously, combining packet spraying with path-aware failure handling that detects problems at hardware speed, [NVIDIA's blog](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/) explains.

The protocol is built into the latest 800 Gb/s network interfaces, [per Network World](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html). NVIDIA describes the failure-handling capability as microsecond-level bypass: when a path fails, traffic is redirected within microseconds rather than waiting for software-based routing protocols to reconverge, [its blog says](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/).

The specification was published as OCP MRC 1.0, NVIDIA noted. Network World [reported](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html) that the OCP submission was accompanied by a research paper on resilient AI supercomputer networking.

## Why It Matters For AI Training

Large model training is sensitive to tail latency: a single late transfer can stall every GPU in a synchronization barrier. OpenAI framed the problem in its own announcement as quoted by [Network World](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html): "Network congestion, link, and device failures are the most common sources of delay and jitter in transfers. These problems get more frequent, and harder to solve, as the size of the cluster increases."

One consequence highlighted by the consortium is topology efficiency. Network World [reports](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html) that MRC's packet spraying lets operators connect more than 100,000 GPUs with only two tiers of switches, versus the three or four tiers needed by standard networks at that scale. Fewer tiers cut component count, power draw and total network cost.

## Hardware And Deployment

NVIDIA says MRC runs natively on its ConnectX SuperNICs and Spectrum-X Ethernet switches, with support for multiplanar architectures that spread traffic across multiple independent fabric planes, [per its blog](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/).

Three production deployments were named at launch: OpenAI's frontier-model training fleet, Microsoft's Fairwater data center described by NVIDIA as "one of the largest purpose-built AI factories for training frontier LLMs," and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure's Abilene site, [according to NVIDIA](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/). Network World [identifies](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html) the OCI Abilene buildout as part of Project Stargate.

Sachin Katti, OpenAI's head of industrial compute, said the rollout was tied to NVIDIA's current GPU generation. "Deploying MRC in the Blackwell generation was very successful and was made possible by a strong collaboration with NVIDIA. MRC's end-to-end approach enabled us to avoid much of the typical network-related slowdowns and interruptions," Katti said in a statement [carried on the NVIDIA blog](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/).

## Industry Context

The announcement is the latest move in a broader pivot from InfiniBand toward Ethernet for the back-end fabrics of AI supercomputers. NVIDIA describes MRC as compatible with existing Spectrum-X Adaptive RDMA and other custom protocols on its hardware, [its blog says](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/spectrum-x-ethernet-mrc/), positioning the company's Ethernet stack as a standards-aligned alternative to its own InfiniBand business for the largest AI customers.

The consortium structure also matters. Submitting MRC to the Open Compute Project — rather than keeping it as a vendor-specific extension — invites Broadcom, AMD, Intel, Microsoft and any other OCP participant to build interoperable implementations on their own NICs, switches and software stacks, [Network World notes](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html).

## What We Don't Know

- The published specification covers transport-layer behavior, but neither NVIDIA's blog nor Network World details how quickly competing NIC vendors will ship MRC-capable silicon outside the deployments named by NVIDIA.
- Performance numbers for MRC versus standard RoCEv2 or InfiniBand at equal scale were not published in the announcement materials reviewed for this article; the consortium's accompanying [research paper](https://www.networkworld.com/article/4168926/openai-led-consortium-seeks-to-address-ai-processing-bottlenecks.html) referenced by Network World may carry such figures.
- AMD's specific NIC and switch products supporting MRC are described on [its own blog](https://www.amd.com/en/blogs/2026/amd-advances-ai-networking-at-scale-with-mrc.html), but were not independently summarized in NVIDIA's or Network World's coverage.
